one_line_definition: A non-contact ultrasonic transducer that uses eddy currents and a static magnetic field to generate ultrasound via the Lorentz force or magnetostriction mechanism.
Closed-form integral expressions for the magnetic vector potential induced by a circular coil above a layered conductor, derived by C.V. Dodd and W.E. Deeds (1968).
A planar coil whose conductors run in alternating parallel lines, used in surface-wave EMATs; pitch set to half the target wavelength for constructive interference.
The force F = J × B acting on eddy currents J in a static magnetic field B, which launches ultrasonic waves in conducting materials.
The characteristic depth at which eddy-current amplitude falls to 1/e; given by δ = √(2/(ωσμ)).
An elastic wave confined near a free surface with both compressional and shear components; velocity in stainless steel ≈ 3.033 mm/µs.
Technique to adapt the circular-coil Dodd–Deeds solution to a straight wire by letting the coil radius → ∞, making the bent wire locally straight.
The angular distribution of radiated ultrasonic energy at a fixed radial distance from the EMAT centre; used to assess array performance.
Numerical method that solves PDEs by discretising space into elements; used here as a reference against which analytical results are validated.
Numerical time-domain method for wave equations; earlier work by the same authors combined FEM or analytical EM with FDTD acoustics.
Inspection techniques (eddy current, ultrasonic, MPI, etc.) that assess material integrity without damaging the test piece.
Auxiliary field A satisfying B = ∇ × A; the primary quantity solved in the Dodd–Deeds eddy-current formulation.
